Location: Near waterfall trail, Samar National Park, Eastern Visayas
Remarks:
Often called Mindanao Crow, this is certainly very different from other subspecies in the Slender-billed Crow complex and, like some other sspp., should be recognised as an endemic Philippine species. 
Presumably a pair, sometimes both calling on the same track in this series, sometimes just one.

Remarks:
1. Recording was made in .m4a format (sample rate: 44.1 kHz, bitrate: 128 kbps) and converted into .mp3 thru zamzar.com. 
2. Habitat: Call was coming from a Santol (Sandoricum koetjape)  tree, from which black-naped orioles are occasionally seen. 
                     Eurasian tree sparrows were also heard at the same time, from the same area.
3. Weather: Sunny with occasional clouds.
4. Bird itself was not seen and identified only through call.
5. Sex unknown.

San Pedro, Laguna, Calabarzon
Remarks: Location is at lakeside with thick vegetation. Recording was captured using iPhone XR, no modification except converting file type to mp3 and cropping out about the last 25 seconds from the original duration. Subject bird/sound was less than 10 feet. Sound came from deep within dense vegetation (grass) almost at ground level. Bird was only seen, at first only a flickering tail, after about 2 minutes since it began its song/call. Bird was eventually seen flying out from cover, made a quick maneuver back to the tall grass before disappearing from view. It was small and brownish, and fast flyer. The song/call resembles that of a Middendorff's Grasshopper Warbler, which is for confirmation.

Bacolod, Negros Occidental, Western Visayas
Remarks:
My neighborhood has a lot of trees and also quite an assortment of birds such as White-collared king fishers, Eurasian tree sparrows, Olive-backed sunbirds, Barred rails, Philippine coucals, Oriental magpie-robins, Yellow-vented bulbuls, and Pied fantails. 

I have never seen this bird land on the ground. It's always about a hundred-feet up the trees. Its beak looked a little thin and a little long, but not as thin as a sunbird's and not as long as a kingfisher's. The beak also looked a little orangey. It was about the size of a thin Eurasian tree sparrow. Its color, as far as I could remember, seemed light brownish. 

They are very frequent at 5:30am to 6:30am, but would still sing in a lot of songs during the entire day. I'm not sure if this was the same bird who had about three voices and mimicked a cat sound "meow." I have also observed that it seems to have 4 to 5 types of calls/songs and each type has 1 to 2 variations (oftentimes in pitch).
